author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
title: The Catholic Faith Concerning Infants.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: What do they say to these things who dare also to write their mischievous impieties, and dare to send them to the Eastern bishops?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Cœlestius is held to have given consent to the letters of the venerable Innocent; the letters themselves of the prelate mentioned are read, and he writes that infants who are not baptized cannot have life.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And who will deny that, as a consequence, they have death, if they have not life?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Whence, then, in infants, is so wretched a penalty as that, if there is no original fault?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: How, then, are the Roman clergy charged with prevarication by those forsakers of the faith and opponents of grace under Bishop Zosimus, as if they had had any other view in the subsequent condemnation of Cœlestius and Pelagius than that which they had in a former one under Innocent?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because, certainly, since by the letters of the venerable Innocent concerning the abode of infants in eternal death unless they were baptized in Christ, the antiquity of the catholic faith shone forth, assuredly he would rather be a prevaricator from the Roman Church who should deviate from that judgment; and since with God’s blessing this did not happen, but that judgment itself was constantly maintained in the repeated condemnation of Cœlestius and Pelagius, let them understand that they themselves are in the position wherein they accuse others of being, and let them hereafter be healed of their prevarication from the faith.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Because the catholic faith does not say that the nature of man is bad in as far as he was made man at first by the Creator; nor now is what God creates in that nature when He makes men from men, his evil; but what he derives from that sin of the first man.
